title: Geopolitics and foreign holdings of euro area government debt
summary: This ECB research paper uses granular Securities Holdings Statistics data to examine whether foreign holdings of euro area government debt react to geopolitical factors. The analysis finds that foreign investors hold almost a quarter of both euro area and US government debt, with total foreign holdings concentrated in geopolitically aligned countries while foreign official sector holdings are mainly held by non-aligned countries. Since Russia's invasion of Ukraine, holdings by non-aligned countries (excluding Russia) have declined by 5% relative to pre-invasion levels, though this decline has been contained and impact on euro area bond yields has been very limited.
key_points: Foreign investors hold approximately 25% (almost a quarter) of both euro area and US government debt
Foreign official sector holdings of euro area government debt are mainly held by non-aligned countries, while total foreign holdings are concentrated in countries geopolitically aligned with the West
Holdings by non-aligned countries (excluding Russia) have declined by 5% relative to pre-invasion levels since Russia's invasion of Ukraine
Geoeconomic risk has come to the fore since Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022
ECB research documents importance of fragmentation for foreign direct investment alongside portfolio investment flows
As of Q4 2024, euro area general government debt securities held by non-residents amounted to approximately €2,591-2,674 billion, with €952.4 billion held by the foreign official sector
Foreign holdings of euro area government debt can affect monetary policy transmission to market interest rates
Impact of geopolitical shifts on euro area bond yields has been very limited thus far
why_it_matters: This research is relevant for understanding the structural dynamics of sovereign bond markets and how geopolitical realignments can affect sovereign borrowing costs. Changes in foreign holdings can interfere with monetary policy transmission and affect the international role of currencies. The finding that geopolitical non-alignment explains part of the decline in holdings by non-aligned countries suggests that further geopolitical fragmentation could have implications for euro area funding costs.
macro_context: Foreign holdings of government debt are a key determinant of the international role of a currency, influencing monetary policy spillovers and shaping the global monetary system. The resilience of foreign official holdings since the Ukraine invasion suggests euro area sovereign bonds have maintained their appeal even amid geopolitical tensions, though continued fragmentation could alter this dynamic.
market_structure_context: The analysis uses granular data from ECB's Securities Holdings Statistics (SHS) covering holdings with euro area custodians. Data limitations include potential undercounting of holdings via non-euro area custodians and deviations from official international investment position data. US TICS data also carry a custodial bias not looking through holdings in European financial centres.
important_numbers: ~25% (almost a quarter) of euro area and US government debt held by foreign investors
5% decline in holdings by non-aligned countries (excluding Russia) relative to pre-invasion levels
€2,591.5 billion in euro area debt securities held by non-residents (i.i.p. data)
€2,674.2 billion in euro area debt securities held by non-residents (SHS data)
€952.4 billion held by foreign official sector
Q4 2024 as the latest data observation
June 2022: end of net purchases of euro area sovereign debt by Eurosystem
notes: Source is a legitimate ECB research publication from June 2025 (part of 'The international role of the euro' series), authored by Roland Beck, Vlad Burian, Georgios Georgiadis, and Peter McQuade. The document was truncated at the character limit, so some data tables and the full analysis section are not accessible. The source is highly relevant to market structure as it examines the composition of sovereign bond holders and how geopolitics affects the investor base. Note: this is a historical record (published June 2025, TODAY is April 2026, approximately 10 months ago), which exceeds the 90-day threshold, so initial confidence has been slightly lowered.
